mission.title: Install and Configure Kubewarden on Kubernetes
mission.description: Kubewarden is a Kubernetes Dynamic Admission Controller that uses WebAssembly policies to validate, mutate, or reject admission requests. Installing Kubewarden requires three Helm charts deployed in sequence: CRDs, the controller, and default policy server.

steps.title: Add the Kubewarden Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Kubewarden Helm chart repository and update your local cache:
```bash
helm repo add kubewarden https://charts.kubewarden.io
helm repo update kubewarden
```

steps.title: Install the Kubewarden CRDs
steps.description:
Install the CRDs chart first — this registers ClusterAdmissionPolicy, AdmissionPolicy, PolicyServer, and audit report Custom Resource Definitions:
```bash
helm install --wait -n kubewarden --create-namespace kubewarden-crds kubewarden/kubewarden-crds
```
The `--wait` flag ensures all CRDs are registered before proceeding.

steps.title: Install the Kubewarden Controller
steps.description:
Install the controller and audit scanner. This chart must be installed after the CRDs chart:
```bash
helm install --wait -n kubewarden kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller
```

steps.title: Install the Kubewarden Defaults
steps.description:
Install the defaults chart, which creates a PolicyServer resource named `default` and optionally deploys recommended security policies:
```bash
helm install --wait -n kubewarden kubewarden-defaults kubewarden/kubewarden-defaults
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check that all Kubewarden pods are running and the default PolicyServer is ready:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubewarden
kubectl get policyservers -n kubewarden
```
You should see the controller, audit-scanner, and default policy-server pods in `Running` state.

resolution.summary: A successful installation will have three Helm releases (kubewarden-crds, kubewarden-controller, kubewarden-defaults) in the kubewarden namespace. The controller, audit-scanner, and default policy-server pods should all be Running. You can then define ClusterAdmissionPolicy or AdmissionPolicy resources to enforce policies.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add kubewarden https://charts.kubewarden.io
helm repo update kubewarden

kubectl get pods -n kubewarden
kubectl get policyservers -n kubewarden

uninstall.title: Remove Helm releases in reverse order
uninstall.description:
Uninstall all three Kubewarden Helm releases in reverse order (defaults first, then controller, then CRDs):
```bash
helm uninstall kubewarden-defaults -n kubewarden
helm uninstall kubewarden-controller -n kubewarden
helm uninstall kubewarden-crds -n kubewarden
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the kubewarden namespace and verify removal:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace kubewarden
kubectl get namespaces | grep kubewarden
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Fetch the latest chart versions:
```bash
helm repo update kubewarden
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade all three charts in order
upgrade.description:
Upgrade the CRDs first, then the controller, then defaults — the same order as the initial install:
```bash
helm upgrade --wait -n kubewarden kubewarden-crds kubewarden/kubewarden-crds
helm upgrade --wait -n kubewarden kubewarden-controller kubewarden/kubewarden-controller
helm upgrade --wait -n kubewarden kubewarden-defaults kubewarden/kubewarden-defaults
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Confirm all pods restarted with the new version:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n kubewarden
helm list -n kubewarden
```

troubleshooting.title: CRDs not registered
troubleshooting.description:
If the controller install fails with missing CRDs, ensure the CRDs chart was installed first and completed successfully:
```bash
helm list -n kubewarden
kubectl get crd | grep kubewarden
```
If CRDs are missing, re-run: `helm install --wait -n kubewarden kubewarden-crds kubewarden/kubewarden-crds`

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
Check the controller logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -n kubewarden -l app=kubewarden-controller
kubectl describe pod -n kubewarden -l app=kubewarden-controller
```
Common causes: missing CRDs (install order wrong), insufficient RBAC permissions, or resource constraints.

troubleshooting.title: PolicyServer not creating pods
troubleshooting.description:
If the default PolicyServer exists but no policy-server pod appears, check events:
```bash
kubectl get policyservers -n kubewarden
kubectl describe policyserver default -n kubewarden
kubectl get events -n kubewarden --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
```

troubleshooting.title: Admission policies not enforced
troubleshooting.description:
Verify the policy is bound to the default PolicyServer and its status is active:
```bash
kubectl get clusteradmissionpolicies
kubectl get admissionpolicies -A
kubectl get validatingwebhookconfigurations | grep kubewarden
```

prerequisites.description: Kubernetes 1.21 or higher (for kubernetes.io/metadata.name label support). Helm 3 and kubectl configured to access your cluster.
